ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKL covers raw bovine meat broadly; no topside-specific FoodEx2 code identified. Closest parent match used as the most defensible anchor available.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw beef topside is approximately 120 g, representing a standard pre-cooked portion for one adult meal.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, topside, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrients and available micronutrients taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for beef topside, raw. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Beef, round, top round, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, raw (FDC ID 174032) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: N/A — Beef Topside, Raw: negligible carbohydrate food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for beef topside, raw. This food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g); pure protein and fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycaemic response and GI testing is not performed on such foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (42 mg/100g) and high potassium (341 mg/100g) yield a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that supports blood pressure regulation.
hypertension.guidance: Beef topside, raw is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt, high-sodium marinades, or sauces; trim visible fat and limit portion to 100–120 g cooked to keep saturated fat intake within cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (21.3 g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef topside, raw is an appropriate protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no carbohydrate load; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-sugar marinades or glazes during cooking.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.4 g/100g) and estimated ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) are present; cholesterol is 37 mg/100g; however, the cut is relatively lean compared to other beef cuts.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may include lean beef topside in moderation (up to 1–2 servings per week, 100 g cooked), trimming all visible fat before cooking, and preparing by grilling, steaming, or roasting rather than frying to minimise additional saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) in red meat amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Limit red meat including beef topside to no more than 1–2 servings per week for rheumatoid arthritis management; trim all visible fat, and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as turmeric, ginger, and leafy greens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef topside, raw, is a lean cut of beef taken from the rear upper leg (hindquarter) of cattle, situated above the silverside and below the rump. It is a boneless, relatively lean muscle cut displaying a deep red colour with minimal intramuscular marbling, though it carries a thin layer of external fat. In raw form it has a firm, dense texture and a mild, clean beef aroma. Topside is widely used across global cuisines for roasting whole, slow-cooking, braising, or slicing thinly for stir-fries and hot-pot preparations. Nutritionally, per 100 g raw weight it provides 163 kcal, 21.3 g protein, 8.6 g total fat (approximately 3.4 g saturated), and 0.0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food with no glycaemic impact. Sodium is very low at 42 mg and potassium is high at 341 mg, supporting a favourable electrolyte profile for blood pressure management. Iron content is 1.24 mg/100g, contributing to haem-iron intake. Cholesterol is 37 mg/100g. The cut is suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and high-protein dietary patterns. It is halal when sourced from appropriately slaughtered cattle. Clinical considerations include moderate saturated fat and estimated ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), which are relevant for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero and naturally zero: beef topside is a pure muscle meat product containing no carbohydrate by composition, confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo database. Sugar_g and dietary_fibre_g are also naturally zero for the same reason.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef topside raw?
Is beef topside good for diabetes?
Is beef topside suitable for keto?
Can I eat beef topside with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per input metadata). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the closest equivalent raw beef round cut. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero and naturally zero: beef topside is a pure muscle meat product containing no carbohydrate by composition (confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo database). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle product. These zero values are biologically expected and not data gaps. Saturated fat (3.4 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.7 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174032 (Beef, round, top round, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available in either HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this specific cut combination): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Glycemic index is not applicable: beef topside contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g); GI testing is not performed on pure protein/fat foods. Halal status is conditional on appropriate slaughter certification. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311434 used as exact match for beef topside. FoodEx2 code A0CKL used as closest_parent match with gap note documented. SNOMED CT code 226934003 used as exact match.

The validator noted 'glycemic_index must be numeric' — null is the correct sentinel for not-applicable GI per the critical q4 rule in the execution prompt, which takes precedence over a numeric requirement for foods with negligible carbohydrates.

Iteration 1: Populated saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g from USDA SR Legacy. Added zero-macro justification in notes. Documented -1 fields.
